name: As'ad ibn Zurara
title: As'ad ibn Zurara (RA): The First of Madinah
meta_description: The story of As'ad ibn Zurara (RA), the young chief who became the first of Madinah to accept Islam, gathered the first Friday prayer, and prepared a whole city for the Prophet Muhammad before he arrived.
keywords: As'ad ibn Zurara, story of As'ad ibn Zurara, first Muslim of Madinah, first Friday prayer, Ansar, pledge of Aqaba, sahaba stories
lede: He was perhaps nineteen or twenty years old. He had no long biography, no books of sayings, and no son to carry his name. He died before the first mosque was even finished.

Yet without him, there may have been no Madinah waiting for the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ at all. This is the story of As'ad ibn Zurara (may Allah be pleased with him): the first of his city to believe, and the one who quietly prepared the ground for everything that came after.

sections.h2: A young chief in a tired city
paras: As'ad ibn Zurara was from Banu Najjar, one of the largest sub-tribes of the Khazraj in Yathrib, the town that would later be called Madinah. His father, Zurara, had died before Islam. His mother, Suad bint Rafi, was from the same tribe.

The people of Yathrib were not like the people of Makkah. Their fathers had worn themselves out in long wars between the two main tribes, the Aws and the Khazraj, and most of the older chiefs had been killed in the fighting. As'ad was not an old man. None of the leaders were. He rose because of who he was: known for his wisdom, his gentle manner, and his good character. He was not hot-headed. He looked for peace and for reconciliation.

He had little attachment to the idols of his people. Some accounts even suggest he leaned toward the worship of one God before Islam, though that is not certain. What is clear is that he had no deep loyalty to the ways of his forefathers. Through his dealings with the Jewish tribes who had settled near Yathrib, he kept hearing them speak of a prophet who was soon to come. The word stayed with him.

He became the chief of Banu Najjar about ten years after the Prophet ﷺ first received revelation. That made him the spokesman for one of the largest sub-tribes in the city, and by extension one of the most respected young men in all of Yathrib. There is a tie here worth noting: the Prophet's own great-grandmother, Salma, was from Banu Najjar. When he later called them his maternal uncles, this was the bond he meant.

sections.h2: An insult that planted a seed
paras: The first time As'ad heard of the Prophet ﷺ, it was not from a believer. He had travelled to Makkah with another man to settle a dispute, and he sought the help of Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, one of the fiercest opponents of the new message.

Utbah spoke of the Prophet ﷺ with scorn. But As'ad listened more carefully than Utbah intended. Behind the mockery he could make out what this man in Makkah was actually calling to: the oneness of God, an extension of the pure way of Ibrahim.

It resembled what those Jewish tribes back home had been waiting for. Sometimes people who set out to turn others away from the truth end up pointing them toward it instead. A seed had been planted, and As'ad carried it back to Yathrib.

sections.h2: Let us be the first to reach him
paras: During the days of Hajj, the Prophet ﷺ would move among the visitors looking for anyone who would listen. He came upon six young men from Yathrib, As'ad among them. He asked if they were the allies of the Jews of Madinah, and when they said yes, he invited them to sit and hear him.

Their answer is one of the most beautiful in the whole story. They turned to one another and said: let us hasten and reach this prophet before the others do. We knew a prophet was coming. Let us be the first to embrace him.

Among six young men, there is always one the rest look to. As'ad was the youngest, yet he was the one they trusted. He spoke what they were all feeling. He was the first of them to extend his hand and his belief to the Prophet ﷺ, and the others followed.

sections.h2: The pledge that severed every other tie
paras: Those six became twelve. The twelve took the first pledge of Aqaba with the Prophet ﷺ and asked him to send someone back with them to teach Islam. He sent Mus'ab ibn Umayr. Together, As'ad and Mus'ab went from house to house in Yathrib. As'ad opened the doors of the city, calmed its leaders, and made the way for Mus'ab to teach, and Islam entered home after home. Some accepted and some kept to their old ways, but the message reached them all.

When they returned the next year, they were seventy. At the second pledge of Aqaba, the spokesman of the Ansar was As'ad himself. He stood and made the cost plain to his people: take this man in, and every tie you have with the Arabs of Makkah is cut. They will all come against you. You may lose everything of this world.

If you mean to walk away, he told them, walk away now, for that is better before Allah. Do not take this pledge and then forsake your prophet. And what do we gain in return, they asked. Paradise, he said. Then he was the first to put his hand in the Prophet's hand, and his people followed him again.
Think of how much rested on the shoulders of a man barely twenty. On that day the Prophet ﷺ chose twelve leaders from among the seventy, and As'ad was appointed the leader of Banu Najjar.

sections.h2: The first to break the idols, the first to gather for Friday
paras: Back in Yathrib there was hesitation. The idols still stood. No one wanted to be the first to touch them. As'ad went to his own people and broke their idols, knowing that if anyone would face the worst of the consequences, it would be him. When nothing struck them down, the other chiefs took courage and did the same.

Then came something the Muslims of Makkah could not yet do openly. The Jewish tribes kept Saturday and the Christians kept Sunday. As'ad was the first to gather the people on Friday, week after week, to remember Allah and to pray together behind Mus'ab. This was before Friday prayer was even formally commanded, and before the call to prayer existed.

They were about forty men, meeting in an open stretch of land, and Mus'ab would lead them in prayer. Years later, scholars would point back to those forty when they spoke of the smallest number for which a Friday gathering could be held. A whole practice of the faith traces, in part, to a single young man who would not wait.

sections.h2: He prepared the city, then he was gone
paras: When the Prophet ﷺ finally made his way into Madinah, he let his camel choose, saying Allah would guide it to its place. It knelt on a plot of land held in trust for two orphan boys. The boys offered the land as a gift, but the Prophet ﷺ refused to take it for nothing and paid them a generous price. That same land was where As'ad had been gathering the people for Friday before the Prophet ﷺ ever arrived. On it the Prophet's mosque would be built.

There is a quiet scene that captures what this meant. A blind old man named Khabib was led to the prayer by his son. Every time he heard the call for Jumu'ah, he would say: O Allah, forgive As'ad. O Allah, have mercy on As'ad. His son had never met As'ad and did not understand. The father explained: the first to gather us for Friday, the first to lead us in it, was As'ad ibn Zurara.

As'ad did not live to see the city he had prepared. While the mosque was still being built, a severe illness took hold of his throat. He died in the very first year of the Hijrah, before the first Ramadan in Madinah, before so much of what he had made possible.
We do not have a single confirmed saying from him. The man who could have told us how all of it began was the one who passed away first, with his work standing unfinished around him.

sections.h2: What the Prophet ﷺ gave back
paras: The Prophet ﷺ washed As'ad himself, wrapped him in three garments, and prayed over him. Many scholars say this was the first funeral prayer in Islam, and certainly the first in Madinah. As'ad was the first to be buried in al-Baqi, the cemetery that would one day hold so many of the family and companions of the Prophet ﷺ.

As'ad had no sons, only three young daughters. The Prophet ﷺ took them into his own household, raised them among his wives, cared for them until they were of age, and saw them married. This was how he answered the loyalty of one who had given so much.

When the people of Banu Najjar came asking who would now lead them, they had no mechanism yet to choose a successor. So they asked the Prophet ﷺ to appoint one. He said: I will be your leader. Some scholars read this as the highest praise of As'ad, that no one could simply step into his place, and so the Prophet ﷺ himself stood in it.

This was the loyalty of the Prophet ﷺ. If you did something for him and you lived, he would point you out and praise you. And if you died and people began to forget you, he would revive your mention. As'ad died early and almost silent, but he was not forgotten. May Allah be pleased with him, forgive him, and gather us with him.

lessons_intro: His name is barely a few lines in the books, yet the whole of Madinah rests on what he quietly built. That is the heart of his story.

lessons.bold: Be the first to move toward good.
lessons.text: When others hesitated, As'ad did not wait to be sure it was safe. He reached for the truth, broke the first idol, and gathered the first prayer.
He prepared a whole city and died before he could enjoy any of it. The work mattered far more than being seen to do it.

lessons.bold: Youth is no excuse to stay small.
lessons.text: Barely twenty, he carried his people on his shoulders. Age was never the measure of what he could give.

lessons.bold: Loyalty is remembered, even when we forget it.
lessons.text: An old man kept praying for As'ad long after others moved on. Good that is done sincerely is not lost, even when its doer is gone.

faq.a: A young chief of Banu Najjar in Yathrib (Madinah) and the first person of his city to accept Islam. He led the early believers there, gathered the first Friday prayer, and prepared the ground for the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ before the Hijrah.

faq.q: Why is As'ad ibn Zurara remembered as a first?
faq.a: He was among the first six of Madinah to believe and the one who spoke for them, the spokesman at the second pledge of Aqaba, the first to break the idols of his people, the first to gather the people for Friday prayer, and the first to be buried in al-Baqi.

faq.q: How did As'ad ibn Zurara die?
faq.a: He fell severely ill with an ailment of the throat while the Prophet's mosque was still being built, and he died in the first year of the Hijrah, before the first Ramadan in Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ washed him, prayed over him, and buried him.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of As'ad ibn Zurara?
faq.a: To be the first to move toward what is right, to do quiet groundwork without needing to be seen, and to give fully even when young. His loyalty was remembered long after his death.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to measure a life by what we get to see finished. As'ad measured his by what he could begin. He believed early, spoke when speaking was costly, broke the first idol, gathered the first prayer, and then died before the mosque was even complete. He never saw the city he made possible.

Most of us will plant things we do not live to harvest: in our families, our communities, the people we quietly help. His life is an invitation to begin the good thing now, to be the first to move, and to trust that sincere work is never wasted, even when no one remembers our name. Carry that into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of As'ad ibn Zurara (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He was barely nineteen or twenty.

No long biography. No recorded sayings. No son to carry his name.

Yet a whole city was waiting for the Prophet ﷺ because of him.

His city, Yathrib, was tired of war.

Its old chiefs had killed each other off. **As'ad rose young**, known for wisdom and a gentle manner.

He first heard of the Prophet ﷺ from an enemy mocking him.

Behind the insult, As'ad heard the truth: the oneness of God. The seed was planted.

Six young men met the Prophet ﷺ at Hajj.

They said to each other: **let us be the first to reach him.**

As'ad was the youngest, and the one they followed.

At the pledge of Aqaba he warned his people plainly.

Take this man in, and you lose everything of this world.

What do we gain? Paradise. Then he gave his hand first.

Back home, no one dared touch the idols.

As'ad broke his own first, knowing the danger would fall on him. Then the others found their courage.

He gathered the people for the **first Friday prayer**, before it was even commanded, before the call to prayer existed.

About forty men, in an open field.

He died before the first mosque was finished.

The Prophet ﷺ washed him, prayed over him, and raised his three orphan daughters as his own.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
The first person in Madinah to accept Islam was not an elder or a seasoned warrior. He was a young chief, perhaps nineteen or twenty: As'ad ibn Zurara (may Allah be pleased with him).

He heard the truth in the words of an enemy, and chose it. He was the spokesman who warned his people of the cost and then took the pledge first. He broke the first idol. He gathered the first Friday prayer. He prepared a whole city for the Prophet ﷺ, and then died before the mosque was even finished, leaving behind no son and almost no recorded words.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/asad-ibn-zurara

email.subject: The first of Madinah to believe was barely twenty
email.alt_subject: He prepared a whole city, then died before he saw it
email.preview: As'ad ibn Zurara broke the first idol and gathered the first Friday prayer, and left no son to carry his name.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

The first person in Madinah to accept Islam left almost nothing behind that we can hold in our hands. No long biography. No collection of sayings. No son to carry his name. He was a young man, perhaps nineteen or twenty: As'ad ibn Zurara (may Allah be pleased with him).

His city, Yathrib, was worn out by war, and most of its old chiefs were gone. As'ad rose young, known for his wisdom and his gentle manner. He first heard of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ from one of his enemies, who spoke of him with scorn. But behind the mockery, As'ad heard what the man was really calling to: the oneness of God. The seed was planted.

At Hajj, he was among six young men who met the Prophet ﷺ. They said to one another, let us be the first to reach him. As'ad was the youngest, yet the one they followed.

Later, standing before his people at the pledge of Aqaba, he made the cost plain. Take this man in, he said, and you will lose everything of this world. If you mean to walk away, do it now. And what do we gain in return, they asked. Paradise, he answered. Then he put his hand in the Prophet's hand first, and his people followed.

Back home, when no one dared touch the idols, As'ad broke his own first, so that any danger would fall on him. He gathered the people for the first Friday prayer, before it was even commanded. He prepared a whole city for the Prophet ﷺ.

And then he died, in the first year of the Hijrah, before the mosque was even finished. The Prophet ﷺ washed him with his own hands, prayed over him, and raised his three orphan daughters in his own home.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: the quiet groundwork can matter more than the harvest we get to see. He began the good thing and trusted Allah with the rest.

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us be among those who begin good without needing to see it finished.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He was perhaps nineteen or twenty years old. He had no long biography, no books of sayings, and no son to carry his name, and he died before the first mosque was even finished. Yet without him, there may have been no Madinah waiting for the Prophet ﷺ at all.

As'ad ibn Zurara (may Allah be pleased with him) was a young chief of Banu Najjar in Yathrib, a city tired of war, where most of the old leaders had killed each other off. He rose because of who he was: gentle, wise, and trusted, with little love for the idols of his people.

He first heard of the Prophet ﷺ from an enemy who mocked him. But behind the scorn, As'ad caught the truth of it: a call to the oneness of God. When he met the Prophet ﷺ at Hajj among six young men, they said to each other, let us be the first to reach him. As'ad, the youngest, was the one who spoke and the one they followed.

At the pledge of Aqaba he stood before seventy of his people and made the cost plain. Take this man in, he said, and you will lose everything of this world. They will all come against you. If you mean to walk away, walk away now. And what do we gain, they asked. Paradise, he answered, and put his hand in the Prophet's hand first.

Back home, when no one dared touch the idols, As'ad broke his own first, so that any harm would fall on him alone. Then he gathered the people every Friday, before the prayer was even commanded and before there was a call to prayer, about forty men in an open field, behind Mus'ab ibn Umayr.

That same open field was where the Prophet's camel would later kneel, and where his mosque would be built. A blind old man named Khabib used to pray for As'ad every time he heard the call for Friday. His son did not understand, until the father explained: the first to gather us for Friday was As'ad ibn Zurara.

As'ad never saw the city he had prepared. A severe illness took him in the first year of the Hijrah, before the mosque was complete and before the first Ramadan in Madinah. The Prophet ﷺ washed him, prayed over him in what many call the first funeral prayer in Islam, and buried him as the first in al-Baqi.

He left three young daughters and no son. The Prophet ﷺ took the girls into his own home and raised them. And when his people asked who would lead them now, the Prophet ﷺ said simply: I will be your leader. No one, it seemed, could be asked to fill the place As'ad had left.
storybook.pullquote: Let us hasten and reach this prophet before the others do.
storybook.reflection: As'ad measured his life by what he could begin, not by what he lived to see finished. He believed early, spoke when it was costly, broke the first idol, and gathered the first prayer, then died with his work unfinished around him. Most of us plant things we will not harvest. His life says: begin the good thing now, and trust that sincere work is never wasted, even when no one remembers your name.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
